coredata.dc:title: Dual photoluminescence emission carbon dots for ratiometric optical dual-mode and smartphone-integrated visual detection of mercury ion
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Dan
Li, Lin
Dong, Xiaorui
Shi, Hu
Wang, Shuo
Zhao, Zhonghua
Lv, Liuliu
Yang, Yongxing
Shi, Lihong
coredata.dc:description: Development of detection platforms having portable, easy-to-use, and cost-effective designs is essential for environmental monitoring as well as food safety inspection. Herein, we proposed a smartphone-based handheld sensing strategy integrated with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) test strip by employing three-dimensional (3D) printing technology for visual detection of Hg2+. Using this handheld detection platform, the FL color of test strips varied from red to purple with the increase of Hg2+ concentration. A great linear response between the R/B value and Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0 μM− 125 μM was shown, with a detection limit of 0.024 μM. Further, the constructed platform was applied for quantitative detection of Hg2+ in real water samples. Interestingly, as-prepared NCDs allowed the monitoring of Hg2+ with the dual-signal ratiometric model. Finally, the NCDs were used to detect Hg2+ in biological systems with satisfactory results. This work will contribute to the new strategy to design easy-to-use and user-friendly devices for on-site quantitation of targets.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ratiometric detection
Handheld device
Hg2+
Imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Handheld sensing platform for visual quantitative analysis of Hg2+
Optical properties and stability of NCDs
Ratiometric detection of Hg2+ in dual-signal sensing model
Handheld detection of Hg2+ in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FT-IR spectrum of NCDs. (B) XPS spectrum of NCDs. (C−E) C1s, N1s, and O1s spectra of NCDs. (F) Hydrodynamic diameter of NCDs. (G) TEM image of NCDs. Inset: Diameter distribution of NCDs. (H) AFM image of NCDs. Inset: Height distribution of NCDs. (I) AFM 3D image of NCDs.
Optical features of NCDs. (A) UV–vis absorption of NCDs. Inset: the images of NCDs under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (B) Excitation-emission spectra of NCDs. (C) FL lifetime of NCDs.

(A) FL spectra of NCDs at various concentrations of Hg2+ within a range of 0 µM − 150 µM. (B) Linear relationship by plotting Ln(I410/I620-0.0928) versus Hg2+ concentration. (C) UV–vis spectrum of NCDs upon the addition of Hg2+ within a range of 0 µM − 140 µM. (D) Linear relationship by plotting A328/A528 versus Hg2+ concentration.
Possible sensing mechanism for NCDs to Hg2+.
(A) Schematic illustration of 3D structure design. (B) Structures of handheld device at different angles.

(A) Schematic illustration of handheld detection process for visual quantification of Hg2+. (B) Images of NCDs-based test strips upon addition of different analytes and different concentrations of Hg2+, respectively (top to bottom). (C) Linear relationship by plotting the R/B value versus Hg2+ concentration.
Hela cells in the presence of NCDs pretreated with Hg2+ for 20 min.
In vivo fluorescent imaging of zebrafish with NCDs in the presence of Hg2+.
Schematic illustration of NCDs for handheld detection of Hg2+ and bioimaging application.
Handheld detection of Hg2+ in tap water and lake water.

head.ce:title: Dual photoluminescence emission carbon dots for ratiometric optical dual-mode and smartphone-integrated visual detection of mercury ion

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, and Institute of Molecular Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, and Institute of Molecular Science, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Medical Sciences, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Medical Sciences, Shanxi Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Academy of Medical Sciences, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan, 030001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Biomedical Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Biomedical Science, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Biomedical Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of detection platforms having portable, easy-to-use, and cost-effective designs is essential for environmental monitoring as well as food safety inspection. Herein, we proposed a smartphone-based handheld sensing strategy integrated with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) test strip by employing three-dimensional (3D) printing technology for visual detection of Hg2+. Using this handheld detection platform, the FL color of test strips varied from red to purple with the increase of Hg2+ concentration. A great linear response between the R/B value and Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0 μM− 125 μM was shown, with a detection limit of 0.024 μM. Further, the constructed platform was applied for quantitative detection of Hg2+ in real water samples. Interestingly, as-prepared NCDs allowed the monitoring of Hg2+ with the dual-signal ratiometric model. Finally, the NCDs were used to detect Hg2+ in biological systems with satisfactory results. This work will contribute to the new strategy to design easy-to-use and user-friendly devices for on-site quantitation of targets.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantitative analysis of target analytes has gained significant attention in the context of food safety, clinical diagnosis, environmental management, agriculture and industrial monitoring [1–3]. So far, numerous quantitative assays have been established, including high performance liquid chromatography [4], electrochemical method [5], fluorescence (FL) probe [6]; [7], and so forth [8]. However, most of conventional analytical methods entail off-site inspection and monitoring, and they require expensive laboratory equipment in combination with highly skilled professionals to operate the equipment. These costly instrument-based quantitation systems are not applicable for real-time and on-site assays. On the basis of the above reasons, development of reliable and portable strategy with fast response, high sensitivity, and simple operation for on-site detection is a primary task, especially in resource-insufficient regions.

Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted increased attention among biosensing researchers because of their unique properties including fabrication simplicity, excellent optical properties, superior solubility, and high chemical stability [9–11]. To date, several groups have reported a series of single-wavelength intensity-modulated CDs-based probes to achieve the detection of target objects [12–15]. However, all face interference from various factors, for instance concentrations of FL probes, fluctuation of light source or detector, instrument sensitivity as well as environmental effects of real samples, resulting in poor credibility. In this regard, ratiometric FL probes may prove to be complementary as they are capable of self-calibration with two well-resolved emission peaks and effectively eliminate the interference induced by external factors, thereby offering more reliable and precise data [16–22]. For example, Niu et al. [23] proposed a bimodal ratiometric colorimetric/fluorometric method using Mn-doped CDs for nitrite measurement in complicated matrices. Gong et al. [24] synthesized 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid-functionalized CDs, which was implemented as a ratiometric optical dual-mode probe for the quantitation of doxorubicin. Wang et al. [25] reported a ratiometric FL sensor based on dual-emission CDs for rapid and sensitive detection of lysozyme. Nevertheless, the investigation on CDs-based ratiometric FL probes for real-time and on-site determination is still at a relatively early stage and there is plenty of space for research.

It is a widely acknowledged fact that environmental pollution caused by heavy metal elements is a global concern to human health even at low concentrations. Among them, mercury ion (Hg2+) is listed in the first category of water pollutants. Continuous or long-term exposure to high levels of Hg2+ can cause damage to the nervous system, kidney failure, developmental disorders, and even death in some cases [26]; [27]. Over the past decades, different detection methods have been developed for Hg2+ measurement, such as cold vapor atomic absorption spectrometry, electrochemiluminescence immunoassay, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, and high-performance liquid chromatography [28–31]. Indeed, most of them are often unsatisfactory that features time-consuming detection, expensive instruments, tedious sample pretreatments. Taking into account these defects, such platforms have received widespread attention for Hg2+ sensing. For example, Zhang et al. [32] designed a smartphone-assisted down/up-conversion ratiometric FL probe to detect Hg2+ in complex environments. Chen et al. [33] synthesized dithiothreitol modified CDs-gold nanoclusters, which was applied for Hg2+ detection in real water samples. Very recently, Chen et al. [34] developed a rotary multi-positioned cloth/paper hybrid microfluidic device for the monitoring of Hg2+. However, as far as we know, no study using handheld detection platforms based on ratiometric FL CDs has been reported for on-site detection of Hg2+.

In this work, we designed a smartphone-based handheld device integrated with ratiometric FL NCDs test strip for on-site monitoring of Hg2+ concentration. Besides, as-prepared NCDs have the capacity to detect Hg2+ with ratiometric manners via the dual-signal sensing model. More importantly, we found that the NCDs can be used to detect Hg2+ in both living cells and zebrafish ( Scheme 1). The innovation points of our study anchor upon the following features: (1) Ratiometric FL nitrogen-doped CDs with simple operation, environmental friendliness, and easy control were first developed for handheld quantification of Hg2+. (2) Handheld detection strategy can overcome the drawbacks of cumbersome sample pre-treatment and reagent consumption in the most of reported CDs-based Hg2+ probes, realizing its great application for on-site detection trial and usage. (3) The device is designed to be portable and sprayed with black paint to eliminate the interference of ambient light, which improves the accuracy of detection. (4) The handheld sensor exhibits a wide linear response within the range of 0 μM− 125 μM, with a lower detection limit of 0.024 μM than other reported Hg2+ sensors. This work is expected to provide a new strategy for on-site and real-time quantitative detection of Hg2+ in resource-constrained environments.

First, 0.1 g of citric acid and 0.02 g of neutral red were dissolved in 30 mL of ultrapure water and stirred well. Next, the resulting solution in an autoclave was heated to 180 °C for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature, the dark red solution was centrifuged (3000 rpm, 30 min) and filtrated by 0.22 µm Millipore filter. Lastly, the filtrate was dialyzed using a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut-off: 500 −1000 kDa) for 18 h and freeze-dried to obtain a solid for further usage.

The Hg2+-sensitive behavior of NCDs was performed at room temperature using phosphate buffered saline (PBS) solution (0.1 M, pH 7). First, 50 μL of NCDs (0.69 mg/mL) was added into 500 μL of PBS. Next, different concentrations of Hg2+ (0 μM−150 μM) were separately introduced into the above mixed solution. After 30 s, the FL spectra were recorded under excitation at 360 nm. The FL intensities of NCDs at 410 nm and 620 nm were noted as I410 and I620, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Handheld sensing platform for visual quantitative analysis of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: Handheld sensing platform consists of three parts: a mini three-dimensional (3D)-printed accessory, the ratiometric FL NCDs-based test strip, and smartphone. The 3D-printed accessory was produced by a It-600 printer. Fig. 6 A shows the process of quantitative analysis of Hg2+ by handheld sensing platform. In brief, a series of FL images can be captured using smartphone camera. And then these obtained images were recognized and transformed into red green blue (RGB) values through color recognizer. Finally, the corresponding relationship between the R/B value and the concentration of Hg2+ was obtained. Preparation of NCDs-based test strips was listed in the Supplementary Materials.

Surface functional groups of NCDs were analyzed by the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR): N–H/O–H between 3296.17 cm−1 and 3487.34 cm−1, CO at 1730.29 cm−1, COOH at 1618.50 cm−1, C–N at 1427.09 cm−1, C–O–C at 1175.66 cm−1 ( Fig. 1A). Further, X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) was performed to study the valence and composition of NCDs. The XPS survey spectrum explains that as-prepared NCDs are mainly composed of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen elements (Fig. 1B), with peaks at 282.12, 397.08, and 530.41 eV, respectively. The three main bands at 284.89, 286.12, and 289.06 eV in the C1s spectra (Fig. 1 C) can be identified as C–C/CC, C–O/C−N, and COOH. The N1s spectra can be divided into 399.15, 400.06, and 401.72 eV, corresponding to C−N − C, N − C3, and N − H (Fig. 1D). The O1s spectra contains two peaks (Fig. 1E), attributed to C−OH (531.98 eV) and CO (532.93 eV). We next detected the particle size of NCDs using dynamic light scattering (DLS). Results show that the mean hydrodynamic diameter of NCDs is around 6.6 nm (Fig. 1 F). Moreover, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) characterizations were used to determine the morphology of NCDs. As-prepared NCDs are uniform and monodisperse, with the average diameter of 1.48 nm (Fig. 1 G). The relatively larger particle size of NCDs obtained by DLS compared to TEM is due to the existence of a hydration layer on the nanoparticles. The AFM image of NCDs reveals their topographic heights in the range from 6.4 nm to 8.2 nm (Fig. 1H−I). Besides, the zeta potential value of NCDs is − 7.29 mV in PBS (Fig. S1), implying that as-acquired NCDs are generally attributed to the abundance of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of NCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and stability of NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2A shows that three absorption peaks of NCDs exist in the UV–vis region. As reported, the broad peaks at 273 nm and 328 nm correspond to the π-π * transition of CC, and it should be noted that the peak at 528 nm is owing to the n-π * transition of CO or C−N. It can be observed in the inset of Fig. 2 A that the NCDs aqueous solution are red under daylight and exhibit strong orange FL under 365 nm UV light irradiation. The NCDs are found to be fluorescent with two emission peaks at 410 nm and 620 nm when excited at 360 nm (Fig. 2B). A quantum yield of 11.2% is derived for NCDs using quinine sulfate as a reference. The average FL lifetime of NCDs is 6.9 ns (Fig. 2 C). Further, we carried out the stability experiments of NCDs at different conditions. As shown in Fig. S2A, a negligible fluctuation on the ratio of FL intensity (I410/I620) of NCDs is noted even if the concentration of NaCl increases to 1 M, indicating that the stability of NCDs is well even in a high sodium ion environment, which is beneficial to their biological application at physiological condition. As presented in Fig. S2B, the value of I410/I620 remains constant at the applicable pH and only shows irregular behavior in the per-acid environment, showing that NCDs have great potential for biological imaging. What’s more, the value of I410/I620 does not change even when the sample is irradiated for 80 min continuously in Fig. S2C. To sum up, as-prepared NCDs show high stability, which will broaden the application scope of NCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric detection of Hg2+ in dual-signal sensing model
ce:section.ce:para: In pure PBS solution, we carried out the selectivity measurement of as-prepared NCDs to various ions by FL spectrometer. As shown in Fig. S3, the value of I410/I620 is significantly increased by the addition of Hg2+ but is unaffected by other analytes, showing high selectivity of NCDs towards Hg2+. For the detection of Hg2+, the optimization of the response time was studied. Fig. S4 shows that upon introducing Hg2+ into the NCDs system, the value of I410/I620 rapidly increases to reach a plateau within about 30 s and remains stable for 20 min. This phenomenon indicates that the optimal response time is 30 s. In addition, the influence of pH was also examined. As can be seen in Fig. S5, the value of (I410/I620)/(I410/I620)0 shows irregular changes with the increasing of pH from 2 to 12 and reaches its maximum at pH 7, implying that the best value is at pH 7. More importantly, this pH value facilitates efficient detection of Hg2+ in living cells.

Next, we carried out the sensitivity of NCDs to Hg2+ under optimized experimental conditions. Fig. 3A shows the FL titrations of NCDs with different concentrations of Hg2+. Upon continuous addition of Hg2+ (0 μM−150 μM), the blue emission peak at 410 nm gradually enhances while the red emission peak at 620 nm gradually reduces. The value of Ln(I410/I620 −0.0928) is linearly correlated to Hg2+ concentration over a concentration range of 0 μM− 150 μM (Fig. 3B), and the detection limit (LOD) is 0.013 μM. Moreover, we have also studied the UV–vis of NCDs to Hg2+. As expected, along with the concentration of Hg2+ increasing from 0 μM to 140 μM, the UV absorption of NCDs at 528 nm diminishes progressively, while that at 328 nm enhances gradually (Fig. 3C). When the concentration of Hg2+ is in the range of 20 μM− 120 μM, there is a good linear relationship between the absorption intensity ratio (A328/A528) and Hg2+ concentration, and the LOD calculated is 0.47 μM (Fig. 3D). These results indicate that obtained NCDs can be used to quantify Hg2+ levels in ratiometric manners via the dual-signal sensing model.

Previous studies have shown that Hg2+ can quench the FL of CDs due to its high binding affinity between CDs and Hg2+, which may facilitate nonradiative electron-transfer that involves partial transfer of electrons in the excited state from CDs to Hg2+. Interestingly, it is found that nitrogen (electronegativity: 3.05) atom is electron donor, and the introduction of nitrogen into the graphitic-like CDs can effectively tune the electronic local density of CDs and promote the coordination interaction between CDs and Hg2+, causing significant FL quenching of CDs system [35]. To understand the binding mechanism of NCDs with Hg2+, we explored the zeta potential, FL lifetime, and TEM of NCDs after treatment with Hg2+. Result shows that the zeta potential of NCDs (−7.29 mV) turns to be + 5.41 mV upon exposure to Hg2+ (Fig. S6), suggesting that Hg2+ can be bonded on NCDs surface. As can be seen from Fig. S7, the FL lifetime of NCDs@Hg2+ decreases to be 6.2 ns when compared with 6.9 ns for NCDs alone, implying an ultrafast NCDs@Hg2+ electron-transfer process [36]; [37]. Fig. S8 shows that NCDs nanoparticles still have good dispersion and the particle size does not increase significantly when Hg2+ is introduced into the NCDs system. Therefore, we can conclude that the FL quenching of NCDs by Hg2+ is owing to the dynamic quenching. It is possible that the oxygen/nitrogen functional groups of as-prepared NCDs can chelate Hg2+ with an empty orbit to form new complexes ( Fig. 4), favoring to reconstruct the NCDs, which results in the FL quenching of NCDs by Hg2+.

For the purpose of taking high-quality images for on-site quantitation, we also fabricated a mini smartphone-linked handheld device via 3D-printing technology, which consists of an imaging dark box integrated with optical elements ( Fig. 5A). The attachment contains four parts: an ultraviolet (UV) light excitation light source, an optical filter, a sample slot, and a removable dark cavity to connect with the phone camera. The four parts plus the rear camera of the smartphone make up the handheld detection platform (Fig. 5B). It is noteworthy that the handheld device includes the following advantages: one is to adjust the shooting distance, making the whole device simpler and smaller. The second is that the mini dark box is sprayed with black paint to eliminate the interference of ambient light.

Next, the FL response towards various analytes was explored by the handheld detection platform. As shown in Fig. 6B, except Hg2+, no obvious changes of NCDs-based test strips happen after adding other analytes, implying the high selectivity of NCDs-based test strip for visual identification of Hg2+ over other analytes. Afterwards, different contents of Hg2+ aqueous solution were dropped on the test strips stained NCDs, respectively. It is apparent that we can distinguish the gradual color change from red to purple with the increasing concentration of Hg2+. The Hg2+ concentration is in the range of 0 μM− 125 μM, and the regression equation is y = −0.018x + 3.529 with the correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.997 (Fig. 6C). The LOD can be as low as 0.024 μM. The above results validate that the smartphone-based handheld device integrated with ratiometric FL NCDs test strip can function as a powerful sensing platform for quantitation of Hg2+. Compared with previous works in Table S1, our designed sensor has wider linear range and lower detection limit, demonstrating that the proposed method is superior to other reported methods for Hg2+ detection. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study in which a handheld sensing platform based on CDs is developed for visual detection of Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Handheld detection of Hg2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: For the purpose of the reliability and applicability of our proposed strategy in the actual samples, the content of Hg2+ in tap water and lake water was detected using the handheld detection platform. As exhibited in Table 1, the recoveries of Hg2+ are in the range from 97.62% to 107.97% and 96.24–102.21% with RSDs of below 4.71% and 4.46%, respectively, indicating that the developed handheld detection strategy can be applicable for visual quantitative analysis of Hg2+ in practical applications.

As is well known, Hg2+ is recognized as a strong carcinogen that can accumulate in the human body, causing seriously harm to creatures and human beings. Thus, it is meaningful and valuable to monitor Hg2+ in biological systems. Before the bioimaging employments, we used the MTT assay to evaluate the cytotoxicity of NCDs. Fig. S9A shows that Hela cells maintain high viability even at a high probe concentration, implying that the probe is suitable for living cells below 200 μg/mL owing to its low cytotoxicity. Next, the photostability of NCDs was investigated in Hela cells. As displayed in Fig. S10, the PL intensity of NCDs-stained Hela cells in the blue and red channels remains almost constant after continuous irradiation for 30 min, which indicates that as-prepared NCDs have excellent photostability and great potential for bioimaging applications. To explore the application of NCDs for Hg2+ sensing, Hela cells were incubated with the probe (100 μg/mL) for 15 min at 37 °C. From Fig. 7, we observed a weak FL in the blue and red channels. With addition of Hg2+, an intense blue FL signal can be clearly observed in HeLa cells, along with a continued decrease of red FL signal. Fig. S11 shows bright field images of NCDs-stained Hela cells in the presence of Hg2+ for 20 min. These results imply that as-prepared probe can efficiently track Hg2+ with ratiometric strategy in living cells.

Further, we carried out the FL imaging experiment in vivo. Here, we selected a zebrafish model that among vertebrates, have a high degree of genetic similarity with humans and hold developmental characteristics generally comparable to humans. First, the survival ability of zebrafish after soaking in NCDs solution was analyzed. No differences in the number of zebrafish larvae exposed to 200 μg/mL NCDs are evident when comparing untreated controls (Fig. S9B), showing that as-prepared NCDs have high biocompatibility. For in vivo imaging, the zebrafish larvae were first soaked in 200 μg/mL NCDs for 3 h. To track exogenous Hg2+ in living animals, we divided the zebrafish larvae into two groups, which were respectively treated with none, and Hg2+ for 30 min. Most strikingly, the zebrafish larvae exposed to NCDs solution exhibit strong FL signals in the blue and red channels. In contrast, Hg2+-treated zebrafish larvae exposed to NCDs solution is significantly different from the untreated cells ( Fig. 8), showing that as-synthesized NCDs is an effective tool for Hg2+ sensing in living animals.

To conclusion, we successfully designed a smartphone-based handheld device integrated with ratiometric FL NCDs test strip for rapid on-site monitoring of Hg2+ levels. Compared with previous sensors for Hg2+ detection, the handheld sensor has a wider linear range and lower detection limit. It is worth mentioning that the handheld detection of Hg2+ based on ratiometric FL NCDs has not been reported. In addition, the handheld sensor was successfully applied for the analysis of Hg2+ in real samples with satisfactory results. Furthermore, the NCDs were used to detect Hg2+ both in vitro and in vivo. We envision this low-cost and user-friendly handheld device as a promising tool for on-site Hg2+ quantitation with minimal sample and instrument requirements.

Dan Chang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Lin Li: Investigation, Validation, Xiaorui Dong: Investigation, Validation, Hu Shi: Investigation, Validation. Shuo Wang: Investigation, Validation. Zhonghua Zhao: Investigation, Validation, Liuliu Lv: Investigation, Validation, Yongxing Yang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Lihong Shi: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874087).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2023.109425.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.
